summary:
Napoleon's Egyptian Campaign (1798–1801 AD) was a French expedition to Egypt and Syria that aimed to threaten British India by establishing French power in the eastern Mediterranean — a strategically ambitious project that succeeded brilliantly on land and failed catastrophically at sea, leaving Napoleon's army stranded in Egypt after Admiral Horatio Nelson destroyed the French fleet at the Battle of the Nile (August 1, 1798). Napoleon defeated the Mamluk cavalry in Egypt at the Battle of the Pyramids ('Soldiers, forty centuries look down upon you') and occupied Cairo, but Nelson's destruction of the French fleet at Aboukir Bay trapped his army in Egypt, unable to receive reinforcements or return home.

The Egyptian campaign's most lasting legacy was scientific rather than military. Napoleon brought 167 scholars, scientists, and artists (the Commission des Sciences et Arts) to Egypt, whose systematic study of Egyptian antiquity produced the monumental Description de l'Égypte (published 1809–1828) — the foundational work of modern Egyptology. The discovery of the Rosetta Stone (1799) by French soldiers eventually enabled Jean-François Champollion to decipher Egyptian hieroglyphics (1822), unlocking 3,000 years of ancient Egyptian writing. Napoleon abandoned his army in Egypt in August 1799, secretly sailing back to France to participate in the coup that made him First Consul — leaving his successors to negotiate a French withdrawal in 1801. The campaign demonstrated both Napoleon's ambition and the limits of land-based power against British naval supremacy — a lesson that would shape his later Continental System strategy.
